title: Types of studies
description: Practice distinguishing between different types of observational studies (retrospective, prospective, sample surveys) and experiments.

assessment_items.question_content:
Employees at a video streaming website suspect that users on desktop computers spend more time on their website than users on mobile devices. They plan on taking a large random sample of users to see if their suspicions are correct.

**What type of study is this, and what is the explanatory variable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In any study, a **response variable** is an outcome being measured, while an **explanatory variable** is used to explain changes in a response variable.

In an **observational study**, researchers do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment.

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

In this context, the employees didn't assign users to groups and make some use mobile devices and others use desktop computers. They simply observed users and noted which device they were using and how long they spent on the website. So this study is an observational study.

They are using the type of device (mobile or desktop) to explain time spent on the website, so the type of device is the explanatory variable, and the time spent on the website is the response variable.

###Answer

This is an observational study where type of device is the explanatory variable.

choices.content: An observational study where type of device is the explanatory variable
An observational study where time spent on the website is the explanatory variable
An experiment where type of device is the explanatory variable
An experiment where time spent on the website is the explanatory variable

assessment_items.question_content:
A study published in the journal *Environmental Health* found that flight attendants get cancer more often than the general population. The $5{,}366$ flight attendants in the study had higher incidence rates for every type of cancer examined, even though they had relatively lower rates of smoking and obesity.

**What type of study is this, and what is the response variable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In any study, a **response variable** is an outcome being measured, while an **explanatory variable** is used to explain changes in a response variable.

In an **observational study**, researchers do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment.

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

In this context, the people involved weren't randomly assigned to become flight attendants. The study simply observed flight attendants and compared their cancer rates to the general population. So this study is an observational study.

They are using the type of person (flight attendant or not) to explain the incidence rate of cancer, so the type of person is the explanatory variable, and the incidence rate of cancer is the response variable.

###Answer

This is an observational study where the incidence rate of cancer is the response variable.

choices.content: An observational study where the incidence rate of cancer is the response variable
An observational study where type of person (flight attendant or not) is the response variable
An experiment where where incidence rate of cancer is the response variable
An experiment where type of person (flight attendant or not) is the response variable

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a study published in the journal *Social Psychological and Personality Science*, people with religious affiliations live longer, on average, than those without religious affiliations. The study analyzed more than $1{,}000$ obituaries and accounted for other variables like sex and marital status.

**What type of study is this, and what is the explanatory variable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In any study, a **response variable** is an outcome being measured, while an **explanatory variable** is used to explain changes in a response variable.

In an **observational study**, researchers do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment.

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

In this context, people weren't randomly assigned to their religious affiliations. Researchers simply observed people's religious affiliations and noted how long they lived. So this study is an observational study.

They are using religious affiliation to explain lifespan, so the religious affiliation is the explanatory variable, and lifespan is the response variable.

###Answer

This is an observational study where religious affiliation is the explanatory variable.

choices.content: An observational study where lifespan is the explanatory variable
An observational study where religious affiliation is the explanatory variable
An experiment where lifespan is the explanatory variable
An experiment where religious affiliation is the explanatory variable

assessment_items.question_content:
Angela created and maintains an exercise app where users log their workouts. She wonders if sending users a daily notification that reminds them to use the app will result in them logging more workouts. She releases an update to her app so that half of users will have daily notifications and the other half won't have daily notifications (randomly determined for each user). After a few weeks, she'll see if one group logs more workouts on average than the other group.

**What type of study is this, and what is the response variable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In any study, a **response variable** is an outcome being measured, while an **explanatory variable** is used to explain changes in a response variable.

In an **observational study**, researchers do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment.

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

In this context, Angela assigned users to groups where one group received notifications and the other didn't. So this study is an experiment.

She is using the presence of notifications to explain the number of workouts, so the presence of notifications is the explanatory variable, and the number of workouts is the response variable.

###Answer

This is an experiment where the number of workouts is the response variable.

choices.content: An observational study where the number of workouts is the response variable
An observational study where notification presence is the response variable
An experiment where the number of workouts is the response variable
An experiment where notification presence is the response variable

assessment_items.question_content:
A recent study showed that viral medications might be useful for fighting certain bacterial infections. Participants with gastrointestinal issues were assigned to either a placebo or viral medication for the first four weeks of the study, followed by an additional four weeks on the other treatment. Their symptoms showed significantly more improvement from the viral medication than the placebo.

**What type of study is this, and what is the explanatory variable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In any study, a **response variable** is an outcome being measured, while an **explanatory variable** is used to explain changes in a response variable.

In an **observational study**, researchers do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment.

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

In this context, researchers assigned participants to groups where one group took the viral medication and the other group took a placebo. So this study is an experiment.

They are using the type of treatment (viral or placebo) to explain symptom improvement, so the type of treatment is the explanatory variable, and symptom improvement is the response variable.

###Answer

This is an experiment where the type of treatment (viral or placebo) is the explanatory variable.

choices.content: An observational study where symptom improvement is the explanatory variable
An observational study where the type of treatment (viral or placebo) is the explanatory variable
An experiment where symptom improvement is the explanatory variable
An experiment where the type of treatment (viral or placebo) is the explanatory variable

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a study published in the *National Library of Medicine*, drinking matcha tea can help with depression. The study involved $190$ mice that were all put in social isolation.  Scientists gave each mouse either matcha tea or a placebo beverage that looked and tasted similar. Mice that drank matcha tea measured significantly lower levels of depression compared to those that drank the placebo beverage.

**What type of study is this, and what is the response variable?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In any study, a **response variable** is an outcome being measured, while an **explanatory variable** is used to explain changes in a response variable.

In an **observational study**, researchers do not assign people to groups or impose a treatment.

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

In this context, researchers assigned mice to groups where one group drank matcha tea and the other group drank a placebo. So this study is an experiment.

They are using the type of drink (matcha tea or placebo) to explain depression level, so the type of drink is the explanatory variable, and depression level is the response variable.

###Answer

This is an experiment where depression level is the response variable.

choices.content: An observational study where the type of drink is the response variable
An observational study where depression level is the response variable
An experiment where the type of drink is the response variable
An experiment where depression level is the response variable

assessment_items.question_content:
Grace was curious if married people were more likely to be safe drivers than unmarried people.

Grace took a random sample of car accident records from the past $5$ years in her country, and she calculated what percent of accidents in the sample were caused by married people. She compared that result with the percentage of all drivers in the country that were married during that time period.

**What type of observational study is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers measure variables,  but they don't attempt to influence responses, and they don't assign individuals to groups or impose a treatment. We can further describe observational studies:

- **Retrospective studies** observe data that already exists for a sample of individuals.
- **Sample surveys** collect data from a sample of individuals to learn about the population at that time. Note that not all sources use the same definition for a sample survey. [[☃ explanation 1]]
- **Prospective studies** follow individuals into the future and record data over time.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22889488db9dca70242c35e014ba5aa2a8447a45)

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

What type of study does this scenario represent?

Grace *observed car accident records that already existed.*

She didn't follow drivers into the future, she didn't collect data from some sort of survey, and she didn't assign people to get married.
This is a retrospective observational study.

choices.content: A retrospective study
A prospective study
A sample survey
None of these. It's an experiment, not an observational study.

assessment_items.question_content:
A famous study examined the smoking habits of British doctors and whether or not the doctors developed various types of cancers. Participants responded to questionnaires about their smoking habits and health in 1951, 1957, 1966, and many more times all the way up to 2001.

**What type of observational study is this?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers measure variables,  but they don't attempt to influence responses, and they don't assign individuals to groups or impose a treatment. We can further describe observational studies:

- **Retrospective studies** observe data that already exists for a sample of individuals.
- **Sample surveys** collect data from a sample of individuals to learn about the population at that time. Note that not all sources use the same definition for a sample survey. [[☃ explanation 1]]
- **Prospective studies** follow individuals into the future and record data over time.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22889488db9dca70242c35e014ba5aa2a8447a45)

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

What type of study does this scenario represent?

The researchers *followed doctors into the future and observed their smoking habits and health over time.*

They didn't use data that already existed, they didn't collect data from some sort of one-time survey, and they didn't assign doctors to smoke or not.
This is a prospective observational study.

choices.content: A retrospective study
A prospective study
A sample survey
None of these. It's an experiment, not an observational study.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of researchers wants to know whether graduates from different teacher preparation programs are more likely to stay in the profession. They select a stratified random sample of this year's graduates from several programs. The researchers will contact the graduates after $1$, $5$, and $10$ years to see whether they are teaching in those years.

**What type of observational study is this?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers measure variables,  but they don't attempt to influence responses, and they don't assign individuals to groups or impose a treatment. We can further describe observational studies:

- **Retrospective studies** observe data that already exists for a sample of individuals.
- **Sample surveys** collect data from a sample of individuals to learn about the population at that time. Note that not all sources use the same definition for a sample survey. [[☃ explanation 1]]
- **Prospective studies** follow individuals into the future and record data over time.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22889488db9dca70242c35e014ba5aa2a8447a45)

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

What type of study does this scenario represent?

The researchers *will contact* the graduates *after $1$, $5$, and $10$ years* to see whether they are still teaching.

They aren't planning to use data that already exists, they didn't collect data from some sort of one-time survey, and they didn't assign people to different preparation programs.
This is a prospective observational study.

choices.content: A retrospective study
A prospective study
A sample survey
None of these. It's an experiment, not an observational study.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vishal was interested in the relationship between a person’s age and their musical preferences. He randomly selected numbers listed in his city’s telephone directory and called those numbers. Whenever someone answered and agreed to be questioned, he asked them for their age and interviewed them about their musical preferences.

**What type of observational study is this?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers measure variables,  but they don't attempt to influence responses, and they don't assign individuals to groups or impose a treatment. We can further describe observational studies:

- **Retrospective studies** observe data that already exists for a sample of individuals.
- **Sample surveys** collect data from a sample of individuals to learn about the population at that time. Note that not all sources use the same definition for a sample survey. [[☃ explanation 1]]
- **Prospective studies** follow individuals into the future and record data over time.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22889488db9dca70242c35e014ba5aa2a8447a45)

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

What type of study does this scenario represent?

Vishal *collected data from a sample of people to learn about their music preferences at that time.*

He didn't use data that already existed, he didn't follow people into the future, and he didn't assign people to age or music groups.
This is a sample survey.

choices.content: A retrospective study
A prospective study
A sample survey
None of these. It's an experiment, not an observational study.

assessment_items.question_content:
The library where Kara works is planning to add a maker space where members can share equipment and services to create crafts. Kara randomly selected members from their database, then contacted them to ask what kinds of equipment and services they would be most likely to use.

**What type of observational study is this?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers measure variables,  but they don't attempt to influence responses, and they don't assign individuals to groups or impose a treatment. We can further describe observational studies:

- **Retrospective studies** observe data that already exists for a sample of individuals.
- **Sample surveys** collect data from a sample of individuals to learn about the population at that time. Note that not all sources use the same definition for a sample survey. [[☃ explanation 1]]
- **Prospective studies** follow individuals into the future and record data over time.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22889488db9dca70242c35e014ba5aa2a8447a45)

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

What type of study does this scenario represent?

Kara *contacted them to ask what kinds of equipment and services they would be most likely to use.*

She didn't use data that already existed, she didn't follow people into the future, and she didn't assign people to kinds of technology or services.
This is a sample survey.

choices.content: A retrospective study
A prospective study
A sample survey
None of these. It's an experiment, not an observational study.

assessment_items.question_content:
Annabella was curious about why people choose to skydive. She randomly selected $10$ flights that were carrying skydivers and interviewed all of the skydivers on the flights about their motivations.

**What type of observational study is this?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In an **observational study**, researchers measure variables,  but they don't attempt to influence responses, and they don't assign individuals to groups or impose a treatment. We can further describe observational studies:

- **Retrospective studies** observe data that already exists for a sample of individuals.
- **Sample surveys** collect data from a sample of individuals to learn about the population at that time. Note that not all sources use the same definition for a sample survey. [[☃ explanation 1]]
- **Prospective studies** follow individuals into the future and record data over time.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22889488db9dca70242c35e014ba5aa2a8447a45)

In an **experiment**, participants are assigned to groups, and at least one of those groups has a treatment imposed upon its members.

What type of study does this scenario represent?

Annabella *interviewed all of the skydivers* on the flights about their motivations.

She didn't use data that already existed, she didn't follow people into the future, and she didn't assign people to flights.
This is a sample survey.

choices.content: A retrospective study
A prospective study
A sample survey
None of these. It's an experiment, not an observational study.
